entries.title: Setting the test scope
entries.body:
# Setting the test scope

Before you start "making noise" across the application, set the **scope** - the boundaries within which you work. This is the first action in any project: scope cuts off extraneous traffic (CDN, analytics, widgets, someone else's domains) and protects against accidental actions beyond what is allowed. In bug bounty, going out of scope is a violation of the program's rules; in a pentest, it is going beyond the contract.

## What a correct scope gives you

- **A clean interface.** The **Site map** and **Proxy > HTTP history** filters can be switched to "in-scope only" - the unnecessary disappears from view.
- **Less junk in the logs.** Burp can be configured to log/intercept only in-scope requests.
- **Automation (Professional).** Live tasks scan only what is in scope; redirects in **Repeater**/**Intruder** are followed only within scope.

## How to set it through the settings

1. On the bottom panel click the **Scope settings** button - the **Settings** dialog will open.
2. Scroll to the **Target scope** section.
3. Under **Include in scope** click **Add**.
4. In the dialog enter the URL prefix you want to include and click **OK**:

```
https://example.com
example.com
example.com:8080/login
```

If the protocol is not specified, both HTTP and HTTPS match.

5. Burp will offer to **stop logging out-of-scope traffic** for the sake of performance - for long sessions it is useful to agree.
6. To cut out dangerous/noisy areas, under **Exclude from scope** click **Add** and enter a prefix (for example `https://example.com/logout`). Exclude takes priority over include.

## How to set it from the context

The fastest way is to right-click any request (in **Site map** or **HTTP history**) and **Add to scope**. On the first visit to a new host, Burp itself will ask **"Add item to scope?"** - agree if the host is yours.

## What to look at

- For subdomains use the **Include subdomains** checkbox (note: during scanning this bloats the amount of work).
- In **Exclude** put everything destructive: `/logout`, mass mailings, payment confirmations, dangerous admin actions.
- Immediately after setting it up, enable the "in-scope only" filter in the map and history - working will become many times more comfortable.

> A precise scope is both convenience and safety. Spend a minute at the beginning so as not to dig through someone else's traffic and not to run into an attack on an unauthorized domain.

entries.title: Mapping the application
entries.body:
# Mapping the application

Mapping is building a model of the target: what hosts, directories, endpoints, parameters and technologies the application has. Without a complete map you test blindly and miss entire sections. This is the foundation of the whole test, so **set the scope first**, then build the map.

## Two directions of work

Mapping splits into two tasks, each with its own approach:

- **The visible attack surface** - what the application itself references. It is uncovered by a manual pass through the browser (passively in **Proxy**) and by an active crawl (**Crawl**, in Professional).
- **Hidden content** - what is not linked anywhere: service directories, forgotten test pages, backups, internal functions, subdomains. It is searched for with separate techniques (content discovery, hostname discovery).

## Where everything accumulates

All discovered content flows into the **Target > Site map** tab - a tree of hosts, directories and endpoints together with requests, responses and (in Professional) the issues found. Sources of population:

- **Proxy** - passively catches everything you open in a browser with proxying;
- **Crawler** - actively crawls links and forms;
- **content discovery / scanner results** - found hidden resources.

## The order of work

1. Set the **scope** on the target host.
2. **Map the visible surface** - go through the application manually via Burp's browser.
3. **Find the hidden** - run the automated content discovery and hostname brute-force.
4. **Analyze** the collected map and prioritize for the test.

## What to look at

- Go through **all roles** (anonymous, user, admin) - the difference in available endpoints points to IDOR and access-control violations.
- **Gray nodes** in the tree are found-but-not-yet-requested links: your TODO list.

> A good map is half the success. The further pages break down each step in detail: the visible surface, hidden content, automated discovery and hostname brute-force.

entries.title: Mapping the visible attack surface
entries.body:
# Mapping the visible attack surface

The visible surface is the endpoints that the target domain references itself: pages, forms, links, AJAX and API requests. The task is to go through the application so that all this content settles in the **Site map**. Before starting, make sure the **scope** is set.

## Manual pass (available to everyone)

1. Open **Burp's browser** (the **Open browser** button on the **Proxy** tab) and go to the target.
2. **Go through the application manually**: click through all sections, submit forms, go through multi-step processes, upload files. By default a **live passive crawl task** is running, which populates the map from proxied traffic.
3. **Do this in all roles** - the public zone, an ordinary user, a privileged one, an admin.
4. Open **Target > Site map** and look at the collected tree.

A manual pass gives the most accurate map: it finds what automation misses - state-dependent content, post-authorization actions, non-standard navigation.

## Active crawl (Professional)

1. In **Site map** right-click the domain's root node → **Scan**.
2. Choose the scan type **Crawl**, and if necessary set login credentials.
3. Launch it and **walk through the application yourself in parallel** in the browser - manual traffic will supplement the automated crawl.

## What to look at

- **Gray (dimmed) nodes** mean a resource is "mentioned in a response but not yet requested." Reach each one: right-click → **Request in browser > In original session**, then open the URL in Burp's browser to get a real response.
- Do not forget the "invisible": API, AJAX, WebSocket, hidden form fields, paths in JavaScript.
- Compare the map before/after a role change - new endpoints for a privileged user are especially interesting.

> Once the visible surface is collected, move on to searching for hidden content and brute-forcing hostnames.

entries.title: Searching for hidden content
entries.body:
# Searching for hidden content

Far from everything that is on the server is linked in the visible part. Hidden content is the resources you cannot reach via links from the application, but which exist and are often poorly protected. It is there that the most interesting stuff often hides.

## What is classed as hidden content

- **Test functionality** that was used during development but was forgotten to be removed.
- **New server functions** not yet connected to the main application.
- **Internal** functions not intended for ordinary users.
- Functions **restricted by the rights** of a particular account (a different role does not see them).
- Service directories, configuration files, backups, old versions, admin panels.

## Two techniques

Burp offers two complementary approaches:

1. **Automated content discovery** - the built-in engagement tool **Discover content** brute-forces typical directory and file names and recursively expands the map with what is found.
2. **Hostname discovery** - searching for additional domains and virtual hosts through manipulations of the **Host** header.

Both methods are covered in detail on separate pages further on.

## What to look at

- Study the already-known structure: directory/file names hint at the naming scheme by which you should guess (`/admin` → `/admin-old`, `backup.zip`, `config.bak`).
- What is found automatically goes into the **Site map** - do not forget to then actually request the gray nodes and analyze the responses.

> Hidden content expands the attack surface beyond what an ordinary user sees. Often it is exactly a forgotten test page or an open backup that gives the first breach.

entries.title: Automated content discovery (Discover content)
entries.body:
# Automated content discovery

The **Discover content** tool (Professional) searches for unlinked directories, files and endpoints by brute-forcing typical names and extensions. It does not just run a single wordlist - it analyzes the already-known structure of the application, derives a naming scheme from it and applies it to new guesses, recursively going deeper into the directories it finds.

## Precondition

First populate the **Site map** with visible content (see mapping the visible surface) - the tool needs a starting structure to build on.

## How to launch it

1. Open **Target > Site map**.
2. Right-click the domain's root node (or the branch you need) → **Engagement tools > Discover content**.
3. The **Content discovery** dialog will open.
4. Click the session status button (**Session is not running**) to start with the default settings.

## What is inside the dialog

- The **Control** tab - start/stop the session and the overall status.
- The **Config** tab - the brute-force settings: which file and directory names to use, the set of extensions, recursion depth, case, etc. You can tune it for the target's technology (for example, add `.php`/`.asp`/`.jsp`).
- The **Site map** tab inside the dialog - a tree of exactly what this run found.

Everything found is automatically added to the main **Target > Site map** as well.

## What to look at

- Review the found nodes by **status codes**: `200` - a working resource, `403` - exists but is closed (also a signal), `301/302` - redirects.
- Tune the set of extensions to the application's stack - otherwise you will miss server-side scripts.
- After the session finishes, return to the main map, actually request the interesting finds and analyze the responses.

> Discover content is noisy by nature - it generates many requests. Run it only within the allowed scope and with an eye on the load on the target.

entries.title: Hostname discovery
entries.body:
# Hostname discovery

Behind a single IP address several virtual hosts often hide: the server or reverse proxy serves different content depending on the **Host** header. By brute-forcing the **Host** values, you can find forgotten subdomains and internal systems that should not be accessible from outside. The technique works even behind a reverse proxy: the requests go to the same IP, and only the specified backend host changes.

## How to do it through Intruder

1. Find a request to the target domain (in **Site map** or **HTTP history**), right-click → **Send to Intruder**.
2. On the **Positions** tab, in the **Host** header enter a placeholder test subdomain, for example `x.portswigger-labs.net`.
3. Select the substituted value and click **Add §** to mark it as a payload position.
4. **Uncheck** the **Update Host header to match target** box - otherwise Burp will overwrite your brute-forced header.
5. On the **Payloads** tab load a wordlist of names (as an example, the documentation suggests the built-in **Directories - short** list).
6. Click **Start attack**.

## What to look at

- Sort the result columns and look for responses whose **Length** or **status code** differs from the baseline - this is a sign that there is separate content for that host.
- Confirm the finds: right-click the interesting value → **Request in browser > In original session**, then open the URL in Burp's browser and look at the real page.
- It is useful to cross this with external subdomain sources (certificates, DNS) - a wordlist does not cover everything.

> Found internal/forgotten hosts are a frequent source of serious finds: they often host outdated or unprotected functionality.

entries.title: Analyzing the attack surface
entries.body:
# Analyzing the attack surface

The map is collected - now you need to understand **where the application is most vulnerable** and plan the test. The analysis goes in parallel with mapping: as you go through, you already gauge what to look at first. The goal is to turn a tree of thousands of nodes into a meaningful audit plan.

## Tools that help

- **Analyze target** (engagement tool) - a summary of the target's size and complexity: dynamic URLs, static URLs, a list of parameters.
- **Proxy > HTTP history** and **Target > Site map** - a quick review of everything Burp has collected about the application.
- **Organizer** - save and annotate interesting HTTP messages to return to them later.

## What the analysis is made up of

The further pages break down specific slices of the surface:

- **Scoping the audit effort** - how much work there is in total.
- **High-risk functionality** - where the valuable/dangerous functions are concentrated.
- **Supported HTTP methods** - which methods the server accepts.
- **Hidden input fields** - invisible parameters, headers, cookies.
- **Evaluating inputs** - how each parameter affects the response.
- **Opaque data** - tokens and encoded/encrypted blobs, decoding and analyzing them.

## What to look at

- Prioritize by "dangerous" features: input points with parameters, file upload, authentication and password reset, object identifiers (`id=`, `user=`), redirects/URLs in parameters, service endpoints (`/admin`, `/api`, `/debug`).
- Annotate finds with highlighting and notes as you go - later it is easy to gather them with a filter.

> Analysis is the transition from "what there is" to "what to attack." The more precise the prioritization, the fewer blind spots and the higher the return on the time spent on the test.

entries.title: Scoping the effort of a site audit
entries.body:
# Scoping the effort of a site audit

Before budgeting time for a test, it is useful to estimate its labor-intensiveness. The audit effort is determined by three factors:

- **the number of dynamic URLs** (with parameters);
- **how many parameters** there are per URL;
- **the nature and complexity of the functionality** (forms, uploads, multi-step processes).

Dynamic URLs almost always require more attention than static ones.

## How to estimate via Analyze target

1. Open **Target > Site map** and select the hosts or tree branches you need.
2. Right-click → **Engagement tools > Analyze target**.
3. In the window that opens, look at the tabs:
   - **Dynamic URLs** - URLs with parameters (the bulk of the work);
   - **Static URLs** - URLs without parameters;
   - **Parameters** - which parameters occur and in which URLs.

The tool shows URL counters, parameter frequency and the associated requests/responses. Important: it analyzes **only the already-mapped** content - populate the map first.

## Other ways to estimate

- **Reviewing the Site map.** A parameters icon on a node indicates a dynamic URL; colored circles (Professional) indicate already-found issues.
- **Live audit (Professional).** It flags complex functionality (for example, file upload) that needs more time to test.

## What to look at

- **Static URLs ≠ a simple response.** A URL without parameters can still serve a dynamically generated response - the classification does not guarantee simplicity.
- The ratio of dynamic to static and the total number of parameters give a rough but honest estimate: many parameters across many URLs - budget more time and, probably, automation.

> Scoping the effort is not an end in itself, but a way to allocate effort: to understand where manual analysis is needed, and where you cannot do without fuzzing and the scanner.

entries.title: Finding high-risk functionality
entries.body:
# Finding high-risk functionality

Not all parts of an application are equally valuable to an attacker. Focus on the areas where a compromise gives the greatest effect:

- the **core functionality** of the application (the very thing it exists for);
- **security mechanisms** - authentication, sessions, access control;
- **special behavior** - error messages, administrative functions, links to external resources.

## How to search

1. **Map the target**, going through the application via Burp's browser.
2. Spot the suspicious in two places:
   - **Target > Site map** (URL view mode) - colored circles indicate issues found by passive scanning;
   - **Proxy > HTTP history** - a list of all HTTP traffic; click items and look at the request/response pairs.
3. For a deeper analysis, enable interception: **Proxy > Intercept**.
4. If necessary, configure response interception: **Settings > Tools > Proxy**, enable **Intercept responses based on the following rules**.
5. On the **Intercept** tab click **Intercept on** and review the messages, modifying them on the fly if needed.
6. Save interesting finds: right-click → **Send to Organizer**; for manual refinement - **Send to Repeater**.

## What to look at

- **File upload** - type bypass, path traversal, potential RCE.
- **Login / password reset / registration** - token logic, access recovery, user enumeration.
- **Admin and debug endpoints** - `/admin`, `/debug`, `/actuator`, service APIs.
- **Error messages** - leaks of the stack, paths, versions, SQL fragments.
- **Off-site links and redirects** - open redirect, SSRF, token leakage in Referer.

> High-risk functions are a short list of targets to start with. One broken authentication mechanism is usually more valuable than a dozen minor information-issues.

entries.title: Determining the supported HTTP methods
entries.body:
# Determining the supported HTTP methods

Due to misconfiguration, the server sometimes accepts methods beyond the usual GET/POST - and they can be useful to an attacker: `PUT` (writing a file), `DELETE` (deletion), `TRACE`, `OPTIONS`, non-standard methods. It is worth checking what is actually supported.

## A single endpoint via Intruder

1. Select a suitable request (usually `GET` to the home page), select the **method**, right-click → **Send to Intruder**.
2. In **Intruder** the method is already marked as a payload position.
3. The payload type is **Simple list**.
4. Add the methods to check: in Professional it is convenient to take the built-in **HTTP verbs** wordlist; in Community enter them manually (`PUT`, `DELETE`, `OPTIONS`, `TRACE`, `HEAD`, `PATCH`).
5. Add a deliberately nonexistent method (for example `XYZ`) to see the "reference" reaction to an unsupported one.
6. **Start attack** and study the responses.

## Many endpoints at once

Use the **Cluster bomb** attack type with two positions:

- position 1 - HTTP methods;
- position 2 - paths (copy them from the **Site map**).

Add a **match/replace** rule to remove the scheme and host from the request line, and **disable URL encoding** for the slashes, otherwise the paths will be distorted.

## What to look at

- **Analyze the behavior, not just the status code.** The server may return `200` to `XYZ` - which means the method is effectively ignored and the reaction is unreliable.
- Check against the **Allow** header (it is returned by `OPTIONS`), but do not trust it blindly: what is actually supported and what is advertised sometimes diverge.
- Found "extra" methods open the way to writing/deleting, bypassing access control or disclosing the infrastructure - check them manually in **Repeater**.

> Support for `PUT`/`DELETE` on the right path can turn out to be a direct channel for uploading a shell or corrupting data - it is always worth a check.

entries.title: Finding hidden input fields
entries.body:
# Finding hidden input fields

Hidden inputs are parameters, headers or cookies that affect the server's response but are not visible during ordinary use of the site. You will not find them in forms and links, yet they often lead to vulnerabilities (from logic bypass to cache poisoning). To discover them, the **Param Miner** extension is used.

## Preconditions

- Install **Param Miner** from the **BApp Store**.
- Set the scope and map the visible surface.

## How to search

1. Open **Target > Site map**.
2. Select one or more requests to check.
3. Right-click → **Extensions > Param Miner > Guess params**.
4. Choose what to brute-force:
   - **Guess GET parameters**;
   - **Guess cookie parameters**;
   - **Guess headers**;
   - **Guess everything!**
5. Click **OK** in the **Attack Config** dialog (with several requests - several times).

## Where to see the results

- **Extensions > Installed > Param Miner > Output** - the run log with all the hidden inputs found.
- In **Professional**, hidden inputs additionally appear as a **Secret input** issue on the **Dashboard** tab - from there you can see the corresponding requests and responses.

## How it works and what to look at

Param Miner compares responses "with a candidate inserted" and "without it": if the responses differ, then the site reacts to this hidden input. Besides the built-in wordlist, the extension learns from words in the responses themselves - the longer it runs, the smarter the guesses.

- A find only tells you that the server **reacts** to a parameter, but does not explain how. The next step is to open **Repeater**, add the found input and change its value, observing the behavior.

> Hidden parameters and headers are a frequent entry into hidden logic and cache attacks. What is not linked anywhere is often also tested the worst.

entries.title: Evaluating inputs
entries.body:
# Evaluating inputs

Once the input points are found, you need to understand **how each input affects the application**: whether it is reflected in the response, whether it changes the behavior, how it is processed. This is the bridge between mapping and the actual vulnerability hunting. Before starting, set the scope, map the application and outline the high-risk functions.

## Three approaches

### 1. Manual evaluation (Repeater)

Send the request to **Repeater** and try variants of a single input one by one:

- **remove the parameter** entirely;
- **give an empty value**;
- **insert a Collaborator payload** into the value.

After each edit, send the request and compare the responses: look for **reflection of the input** in the body, a difference in **behavior** and in **response time**. Subtle differences between two responses are convenient to highlight in **Comparer**.

### 2. Scanning (Professional)

Select an input, right-click → **Scan selected insertion point**. Or send the request to **Intruder**, mark several inputs as positions and run **Scan defined insertion points**. The results are on **Dashboard > Issues**.

### 3. Fuzzing (Intruder)

Send the request to **Intruder**, mark the inputs as payload positions, add fuzzing strings (in Professional - the built-in **Fuzzing - full** lists) and run the attack. Study the anomalies in the responses.

## What to look at

- **Reflection of the input** - a candidate for XSS/injections into the response.
- **A difference in response time** - a hint of blind injections (SQLi, command injection).
- **A change in the application's state** from an empty/removed parameter - logic flaws, bypassing checks.
- **Collaborator hits** - an out-of-band interaction (SSRF, blind injection, leaks).

> The goal of the stage is not to "break" but to understand the nature of each input. Knowing how a parameter reaches the response, you precisely choose the class of attack instead of blind brute-forcing.

entries.title: Analyzing opaque data
entries.body:
# Analyzing opaque data

Opaque data is values that are **not human-readable** because they are encoded or encrypted. Typical examples are session tokens and anti-CSRF tokens: the server uses them to verify the integrity and authenticity of data. For an attacker it is important to understand **what is inside** and **what can be safely changed**.

## Why analyze opaque data

- Inside a token there may turn out to be meaningful content: a username, an identifier, a role, a timestamp, an expiration.
- If the value is editable and the server validates it weakly - this is a path to session forgery, privilege escalation, bypassing CSRF protection.

## The general order of work

Burp provides the means to work with such data more conveniently. The methodology comes down to two steps:

1. **Decode** - bring the value to a readable form (see the separate page on decoding). Tools: **Inspector** (automatically recognizes URL/Base64 right in the message) and **Decoder** (a wide set of transformations, including multi-layered ones).
2. **Modify and observe** - change the decoded content, re-send the request (via **Repeater**) and see how the response changes. Subtle differences are convenient to catch in **Comparer**.

A separate technique is to determine **which exact parts of the token affect the response** (see the next page): this shows what in the token is significant and what is ballast.

## What to look at

- Look in the decoded form for familiar pieces: `username`, `id`, `role`, timestamp, separators (`.`, `:`, `|`).
- A structure of three parts separated by dots is almost certainly a **JWT**; analyze it as a separate format.
- If the value does not decode with anything - it may be encrypted; then a part-by-part analysis (frobber/bit flipper) is useful.

> Opaque does not mean secure. Many tokens only "look" protected; having taken apart the structure, you often find editable fields that the server trusts in vain.

entries.title: Decoding opaque data
entries.body:
# Decoding opaque data

To understand what lies inside a token or an encoded parameter, it must be brought to a readable form. In Burp there are two main tools for this: **Inspector** (fast, right in the message) and **Decoder** (flexible, for complex multi-layered encodings).

## Through Inspector

Inspector automatically recognizes typical encodings (URL, Base64) and shows the decoded form next to the original, allowing you to edit it in place.

1. Find the opaque value in **Proxy > HTTP history**.
2. Select the encoded text - the decoded version will appear in **Inspector** automatically.
3. If you need to change and re-send - send the request to **Repeater**.
4. Edit the decoded data in the **Inspector** panel.
5. Click **Apply changes** - Burp re-encodes the value and updates the request.
6. Send the request and assess the effect on the response.

## Through Decoder

Decoder supports more functions and can apply transformations sequentially - this is needed when the data is encoded in several layers (for example, URL-encoded over Base64 over gzip).

1. Right-click the data in **Proxy > HTTP history** → **Send to Decoder**.
2. Click **Decode as** and choose a function (**Base64**, **URL**, **ASCII hex**, **Gzip**, etc.).
3. For multi-layer encoding, apply **Decode as** to the result again, until you get a readable form.
4. Edit the decoded data.
5. Click **Encode as** on the final panel and apply the encodings **in reverse order** to assemble the value back.
6. Copy the repacked value back into the original request.

The **Smart decode** button is also useful - Burp itself tries to guess the encoding and unwind the layers.

## What to look at

- Look for meaningful pieces: usernames, timestamps, identifiers, role flags.
- Match the structure against known formats (Base64 often ends with `=`; hex is only `0-9a-f`).
- Decoded it, understood the format - move on to checking which parts actually affect the response.

> The goal of decoding is not the text itself, but understanding the mechanics: what the application stores in the token and which of these fields you can substitute.

entries.title: Which parts of a token affect the response
entries.body:
# Which parts of a token affect the response

Even without knowing the internal format of a token, you can find out **which of its parts are significant** and which are not. The idea is simple: change one character (or bit) at a time and watch at which moment the server starts reacting differently. For this, **Intruder** has special payload types.

## How to do it

1. Find the message with the target token (for example, a session cookie), right-click → **Send to Intruder**.
2. In **Intruder** the token is already marked as a payload position.
3. Choose the payload type:
   - **Character frobber** - takes the base value and changes each character in turn, increasing its ASCII code by one. Suitable for most cases.
   - **Bit flipper** - toggles individual bits; needed for finer control, primarily when the token is encrypted with a **block cipher in CBC mode** (changing bits in the previous block predictably affects the decrypted data of the next one).
4. Add extraction from the response: in the **Grep - Extract** section click **Add**, select a significant part of the response (best of all - an error message) and click **OK**.
5. Click **Start attack** - Intruder sequentially changes each character/bit position.

## What to look at

- Compare by the **Grep - Extract**, **status** and **response length** columns: where the response changed - that character/bit is **significant** (participates in the check); where it stayed the same - the part is most likely not used.
- The key sign: "if you changed a character in the session token and the request is still processed within your session - that character is probably not used for tracking the session."
- For encrypted CBC tokens, switch from frobber to **Bit flipper**: this way you can feel out the editable fields inside the ciphertext.
- Subtle differences between two responses are convenient to additionally analyze in **Comparer**.

> This technique shows the "skeleton" of the token: which bytes hold the value, and which are padding/noise. Knowing the significant fields, you precisely attack exactly them, instead of brute-forcing the whole token.
